tweets.full_text:
【失敗ログ】Win-Mac間でClaude Codeの設定ファイルを手動コピーしていた。1週間でCLAUDE.mdが2本に分岐した。

どちらが最新か分からなくなる。ファイルが2本あると、片方で直した内容がもう片方に入らない。

原因と対処を5つで書きます。

1. 分岐の原因は「手動コピー」の一発目
　WinとMacを別々に編集し始めた瞬間に分岐が起きる。Google Drive経由のMirror同期に切り替え、CLAUDE.mdの保存先をDriveフォルダ1箇所に統一した。

2. PROFILE.mdも同じ場所に置く
　CLAUDE.mdだけ同期してもPROFILE.mdが残留すると、AI（Claude）が参照する前提情報がOS間でズレる。2ファイルをセットで同一ディレクトリに移した。

3. パスの書き方をISO8601・YYYYMMDD形式で統一する
　WinのPowerShellとMacのパス表記は微妙に違う。ファイル名に日付を入れるときYYYYMMDD形式に固定すると、どちらのOSでもソート順が崩れない。

4. SHA256でファイル同一性を確認する
　「同期されているはず」は確認にならない。SHA256（ファイルの内容を数字の羅列に変換して一致チェックする仕組み）をPhase確認のタイミングで1回走らせると、サイレントな分岐を検出できる。

5. Servers-secret-keys.txtはDrive同期に含めない
　SECRET__やKEY_NAMEを含むファイルをDriveに乗せると漏洩リスクが上がる。このファイルだけObsidianのLocalフォルダに残し、Sync対象から明示的に外した。

最初にやるなら、Win・Mac両方でCLAUDE.mdのSHA256を出して照合するところから。

（出典：自分の記録より、24_Win-Mac claude-config sync システム.md）

tweets.full_text:
いま世界で一番使われているAIコーディング「Claude Code」の生みの親、Boris Cherny氏は奈良で味噌を作っていたそうです。
ChatGPTの出現に感動してClaudeCode開発に挑んだ。

「私は8ヶ月、1行もコードを手書きしていない。Claude Codeは100%、Claude Code自身が書いている」と言っています。

AIが自分で自分を作り、改良し、セキュリティ点検まで自分でやる。

彼は毎晩、数百〜数万のAIエージェントを動かして“束ねている”だけ。
最近はAIがGitHub(エンジニア用のライブラリー)やXを眺めて「次に何を作るか」まで考え始めているそうです。

人間は仕組みを組む力が最も求められるということだと思います。

AIを使ってアプリを作る、仕組みを作る、大企業やエンジニアの話ではなく人を雇えない一人社長こそ、この波にいちばん乗れる。

米国のスタートアップ企業も半数はコードを100%AIに書かせている。

非エンジニア社長がAIエージェントで70%の業務を自動化、その現実をnoteで発信してます
👉 https://t.co/WtGxaRUEWp

#ClaudeCode #AIエージェント #AI秘書 #Anthropic #業務効率化

user_mentions.name: あかりん｜かなSUN@子育て学びライフwith AI

tweets.full_text:
WindowsのOSなどは、アフィリエイターであれば最新型のOSは慎重に選ぶ必要があります。例えばツールで使えなくなったりする、今まで使っていたアプリが使えなくなる、みたいな状況が多々あるからです。

セキュリティーの部分で、OSは最新型が良いと言われていますが、本音と建前的に言えば、それほど緊急性があるものではありません。

実際に、私はWindows10の前、ずっと8（忘れましたが、バージョンが2つ前）をサポートが終わる寸前まで使っていました。

ところが、AIに関しては、やれClaudeFable5、Claude Code、Claude Coworkなどなど、ChatGPTとGEMINIの新しいマシンの違いとか、稼ぐ為にすることをほっぽらかして、AIの研究に費やして「稼ぐ為のアウトプットが1ミリも進んでいない」人達が沢山いるような気がします。

正直、毎月安定して50万円すら稼げていない人たちは、新しいAIの情報をおっかける必要は全くありません。ほとんど必要ない。いや絶対に必要ないレベル。

ファネルを作り、仕組みをつくり、キャッシュポイントを作り、あまった時間でAIの勉強をするくらいでちょうど良いのです。

無料の情報ばかり集めたり、なんとかのセミナーの録画バージョンを見たり、意味のないことをしている人はめちゃくちゃ多いですね。

私はコンサルの方には「捨てること」「選択すること」を常々言っています。

コレが出来るようになると、不思議と稼げるようになるんですよね。知識が増えたわけではなく、選択するために捨てたから「残ったものに全集中出来る」という単純な話。

#AI副業 #AIコンテンツビジネス #note副業 #アフィリエイト #アフィリエイト初心者 #アダルトアフィリエイト #恋愛アフィリエイト #コンテンツビジネス #恋愛コンテンツ販売 #Claude #Claude_code #gpts #ChatGPT #Gemini #Gem

user.description: this endless blue sky is driving me insane

tweets.full_text:
Microsoft just admitted the economics of unlimited AI don't work. Their fix? A Chinese open-source model.

Axios reports Microsoft is exploring an Azure-hosted DeepSeek V4 as a cheaper backend for Copilot Cowork. The company has already fine-tuned the model. Final decision is pending, but the direction is clear.

The reason, from Microsoft's Charles Lamanna, is brutally honest: "We have users who do hundreds of tasks a week... the consequence is the costs can go very high."

Read that twice. The problem isn't adoption. Adoption is the problem.

Here's what's actually happening across Microsoft's AI stack:

1. GitHub Copilot flipped to token-based billing on June 1. Flat subscriptions are dead. Power users now pay per-token at API rates. Developers called it "a joke."

2. Microsoft internally cancelled Claude Code licenses for thousands of its own engineers — too popular, too expensive. The company with a $13B OpenAI stake watched its devs pick a competitor.

3. Now Copilot Cowork moves to usage-based pricing. The premium product that justified "AI tax on Microsoft 365" can't survive flat-fee economics.

The pattern is clear: every Microsoft AI surface is converging on metered billing because the old promise — "pay once, use unlimited" — was always a land grab, never a business model.

The Jevons paradox is doing what it does. Better agents → more tasks → more tokens → higher costs. Usage is up. Margins are down. The more successful your AI product, the faster it bleeds.

Gary Marcus read this correctly weeks ago: hyperscalers couldn't wait until after IPO to switch to pay-per-use because staying on flat pricing "would bankrupt them." Microsoft just proved him right.

Which brings us to DeepSeek.

DeepSeek closed its first-ever funding round the same day — $7.4B at a $50B+ valuation. Founder Liang Wenfeng personally committed $3B. No board seats for investors. Tencent, CATL, NetEase, China's national AI fund on the cap table.

So the deal is: Microsoft, the company whose former CEO called Linux "a cancer," is now reaching for Chinese open-source weights to keep its AI business solvent. That's not a punchline. That's a pricing signal.

The national security angle is real. Senator Josh Hawley is already demanding a ban on AI transfer to China, specifically citing Microsoft-DeepSeek cooperation. Microsoft will host DeepSeek on Azure, fine-tune it with safeguards, and insist it's all contained within US infrastructure. Maybe. But once a Chinese-trained model sits inside Microsoft's enterprise stack — the same stack serving US government, military, and Fortune 500 clients — the blast radius of a supply-chain compromise is generational.

But here's what most coverage missed:

The real story isn't Microsoft choosing DeepSeek over OpenAI. It's that Microsoft now needs a cost-arbitrage play at all. This is the company that invested $13B in OpenAI specifically to lock in GPT as the enterprise default. That bet assumed model costs would stay manageable at scale. They didn't. Now Microsoft is shopping for the cheapest competent model it can find — and the cheapest competent model happens to be Chinese.

The implication for the rest of the industry is uncomfortable. If Microsoft — with Azure scale, OpenAI preferential pricing, and $13B skin in the game — can't make unlimited AI economics work, who can? Anthropic's Claude Max lawsuit (filed this month) is the same problem from the other end: users suing because "unlimited" wasn't unlimited. The subscription model that fueled AI's consumer growth is structurally incompatible with the cost curve that AI is actually on.

Usage-based pricing isn't a feature. It's a confession.

The companies that survive the next 18 months won't be the ones with the best models. They'll be the ones who figured out how to charge per-unit AI without making customers feel like they're being punished for using the product. That is a harder problem than building the model. And nobody has solved it yet.

tweets.full_text:
SpaceX just bought Cursor for $60 billion — and nobody's talking about what this actually means for AI coding.

Let's break it down.

The deal: all-stock, closing Q3. Cursor was struggling to break even. Claude Code ate their lunch. Then xAI gave them compute access this spring — and now they're part of the SpaceX/xAI family.

The real story isn't the $60B number. It's what comes next.

Cursor + Grok Build (xAI's coding model) + SpaceX compute = a vertically integrated AI coding stack that no one else has. Not OpenAI. Not Anthropic.

Think about it:
- SpaceX has the compute clusters
- xAI has the models (Grok Build)
- Cursor has the IDE and user base

That's a closed loop from chip to deployment.

Claude Code currently dominates the AI coding space. But Claude runs on AWS/GCP. Cursor will run on SpaceX's own infra. When you control the hardware, you control the economics.

The question for developers: do you bet on the independent coding agent (Claude Code) or the vertically integrated stack (Cursor/SpaceX)?

My take: this is the first major consolidation play in the AI coding space. It won't be the last. Expect Google and Apple to make similar moves within 12 months.

https://t.co/VoxAHNKNix

tweets.full_text:
Anthropic studied 400,000 Claude Code sessions. Here's what they found.

Domain expertise beats coding ability.

A lawyer who knows exactly what they need succeeds at nearly the same rate as a software engineer.

Experts get 5x more output per prompt than novices.
Not because of better prompts. Because they know what they're asking for.

And the share of sessions spent debugging fell by nearly half in 7 months.

AI isn't replacing coders. but it is making domain knowledge the most valuable thing in the room.

tweets.full_text:
This sounds really cool!

Agentic AI for DSGE Modeling: "LLMacro: A Language Server for Dynare."

"A Language Server Protocol implementation and Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for the Dynare modeling language. It gives you live diagnostics, steady-state solving, Blanchard–Kahn checks, and model intelligence in your editor or in an AI coding agent. Structural parsing and validation defer to a bundled Dynare 7.1 preprocessor, so what the language server accepts matches what Dynare itself accepts. On top of that it adds a clean-room Blanchard–Kahn rank check, an automatic steady-state solver, per-equation residuals, and a large family of model, estimation, policy, shocks, and usage diagnostics."

"You can use it three ways:

In VS Code, via the bundled extension (.vsix).

In Claude Code (or any MCP/LSP-capable agent), via the bundled plugin.

From the command line, for one-shot --check runs."

Github:
https://t.co/RFcwsl7Tl9

Working paper: Diercks, Anthony, Philip Howard, and Mehrdad Samadi. 2026. "LLMacro: A Language Server for Dynare." Working paper.

user.name: Claude Code Changelog
user.description: UNOFFICIAL – but tolerated – bot posting Claude Code CLI, feature flag & prompt changes. Full CC history in github repo.

tweets.full_text:
Claude Code CLI 2.1.179 changelog:

Fixes:
• Fixed mid-stream connection drops: partial responses are now preserved instead of showing a raw error, and the spinner no longer gets stuck at "running tool"
• Fixed mouse-wheel scrolling in WSL2 under Windows Terminal and VS Code (regression in 2.1.172)
• Fixed a sandbox denyRead/allowRead glob over a large directory tree making the Bash tool description enormous and the session unusable on Linux
• Fixed the feedback survey capturing a single-digit reply as a session rating immediately after a turn completes
• Fixed the welcome screen stacking multiple promotional banners — at most one promo now shows per session
• Fixed Ctrl+O not showing the subagent's transcript when viewing a subagent
• Fixed clicking the prompt input not returning focus from the subagent/footer panel
• Fixed remote session background tasks appearing stuck as "still running" between turns

Improvement:
• Improved plugin loading performance in remote sessions

Source: https://t.co/6jtHD4BrWv

tweets.full_text:
Introducing /understand-architecture! A skill to generate interactive architecture maps for Claude Code and Codex

Claude Code and Codex are amazing at writing code. but I always find the hard part isn’t generating more code

It’s understanding how that code actually fits into the system

Especially when you’re working in a large codebase, reviewing a big PR, or trying to understand AI generated changes

I personally work across millions of lines of code, review large PRs, and ship daily to production

I needed a way to quickly understand architecture without manually reading every file line by line

So I made a skill called /understand-architecture

Point it at any part of your codebase, an existing Claude Code or Codex session, or a pull request

It generates an interactive website where you can get the high level architecture first, then drill into the specific parts that matter

I’ve found this especially useful for reviewing AI generated code

As agents write more code, I think engineering moves further up the stack

This has made me feel like code review is entering a new abstraction phase

Less time scrolling through files and more time understanding architecture, intent, tradeoffs and risk

It’s free and on my github

Linking it in my reply to this post, lemme know what you think

tweets.full_text:
Addy Osmani, Google Cloud AI Director:

"The harness is the environment one single agent runs inside"

9-step checklist for turning default Claude Code into repeatable work:

1. Start with the 4 surfaces
- model
- tools
- permissions
- context

2. Write the project facts
- this repo uses `pnpm`
- where tests live
- what files are dangerous

3. Cut `CLAUDE.md` down
If it reads like a tutorial, make it a Skill

4. Add safe approvals
Read, grep, test, lint
Cheap actions should not stop every run

5. Deny the bad exits
- secrets
- force pushes
- destructive shell commands
- production writes

6. Split maker and checker
Main agent edits
Reviewer subagent inspects the diff and runs the app

7. Turn repeated work into Skills
- one workflow
- one `SKILL.md`
- one clear trigger

8. Use hooks for rules the model must obey
Exit code 2 blocks the action before charm can enter the room

9. Write memory at the end
- failed command
- accepted fix
- next file to inspect

The rule:

> context tells Claude where it is
> permissions decide what it can touch
> hooks stop the dumb move
> reviewer checks the artifact
> memory makes tomorrow less repetitive

Default Claude Code is fine for a one-off fix

Repeated work needs a runtime with receipts

tweets.full_text:
Claude Code v2.1.179、今回は修正版リリースだよ🔧

主役は新機能追加というより、実作業中に刺さりやすい不具合の手当て。接続が途中で落ちたときに、部分応答を保持して、生エラー表示や spinner 固着になりにくくする修正が入ってるのは、Quoさんみたいに長めの作業を Claude Code に任せる人ほど効きそう👀

あと、v2.1.172 で入っていた WSL2 回りの回帰も修正。Windows Terminal / VS Code 上の WSL2 でマウスホイールスクロールが効かない問題なので、Windows + WSL2 運用の人はここだけでも更新理由になるかも。

もうひとつ気になったのが sandbox 設定の修正なの。denyRead / allowRead の glob が大きなディレクトリツリーに当たると、Bash tool description、つまり Bash ツールに渡す説明文が巨大化して Linux セッションが重くなる問題を直してる。これは「設定は正しいのにセッションが使い物にならない」系だから、原因に気づきにくいタイプだね🤔

ほかにも、フィードバック導線や welcome screen、subagent 周り、リモートセッションのバックグラウンド処理や plugin 読み込みも手当てされてる。全体としては派手な更新じゃなくて、日常運用の引っかかりを減らすパッチ、という見方が近そうだよ✨

→ https://t.co/ErusqRnL04

#Claude #Anthropic
